title: Hummus
subtitle: Smooth chickpea and tahini dip with olive oil and paprika
excerpt: The real thing: dried chickpeas soaked overnight and cooked long, blended with a generous amount of tahini, garlic, and lemon into a pale, creamy dip. The overnight soak and the long cook are not optional if you want a smooth result. Serve at room temperature with olive oil pooled on top and a scatter of paprika.
sourceNotes: Hummus bi tahini is one of the foundational dishes of Levantine cuisine, documented in Arab cookbooks from the medieval period and in wide use across Lebanon, Syria, Palestine, Jordan, and Egypt. Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968) and Anissa Helou's 'Feast' (2018) are the primary references. The technique of using dried chickpeas and a high tahini-to-chickpea ratio is the Levantine standard.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the batch and keep in the fridge for up to 4 days. Dress each portion fresh with olive oil.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a day ahead — the flavour improves. Bring to room temperature before serving and add fresh olive oil.

content.text: The quality gap between hummus made with dried chickpeas and hummus made with tinned chickpeas is large enough that it is worth knowing about. Tinned chickpeas produce a workable hummus but they lack the depth and body of a long-cooked chickpea. Dried chickpeas, soaked overnight and cooked for 90 minutes until very soft, blend into something noticeably smoother and richer. The method is not difficult — it just requires planning.

The tahini ratio matters. Most restaurant hummus is heavy on chickpeas and light on tahini, which produces a beige paste. The Lebanese home version uses tahini almost as generously as chickpeas, and the result is pale, slightly bitter, and complex in a way that the lighter version is not. Add the ice-cold water last to loosen — it makes the texture light rather than dense.

items.prepNote: crushed to a paste with salt

content.text: Drain the soaked {{chickpeas-dried}} and cover with fresh cold water in a large saucepan. Bring to the boil, skim any foam, then reduce the heat and simmer for 60 to 90 minutes until very tender — they should mash easily between two fingers. Drain, reserving the cooking water. Leave to cool slightly.

Reserve a handful of whole chickpeas for garnish if you like. Place the rest in a food processor with the {{tahini}}, {{garlic}} paste, and {{lemon}} juice. Blend for 2 minutes. With the machine running, add 4 to 6 tablespoons of the reserved cooking water (or ice-cold water), a spoonful at a time, until the hummus is pale, smooth, and creamy. Taste and adjust salt and lemon. Blend for another minute — longer blending makes it lighter.

Spread onto a plate, creating a hollow in the centre with the back of a spoon. Pour {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} into the hollow. Scatter {{paprika-sweet}} over the top. Serve at room temperature with warm pitta or flatbread.

items.symptom: Hummus is grainy rather than smooth
items.cause: Chickpeas not cooked long enough, or not blended long enough
items.fix: Return the chickpeas to the pot and cook for another 20 minutes — they should be very soft, almost falling apart. Then blend for at least 3 minutes in total, adding water gradually to loosen

items.symptom: Hummus is too dense and stiff
items.cause: Not enough liquid added during blending
items.fix: With the processor running, add cold water a tablespoon at a time until the texture loosens to a smooth, spreadable consistency

items.cause: Not enough lemon, salt, or tahini
items.fix: Taste the hummus before adding water — it should be assertively seasoned. Add lemon juice first (it brightens without changing the texture), then salt

items.cause: Too much raw garlic or garlic not crushed to a paste
items.fix: Crush the garlic with salt to a smooth paste before adding — raw chunks of garlic create pockets of sharpness rather than even heat throughout

content.text: Hummus is claimed by every country in the Levant and disputed between all of them, which gives some indication of how central it is to the regional food culture. The word means 'chickpeas' in Arabic; the full name hummus bi tahini means chickpeas with tahini. Its appearance in Arab cookbooks goes back at least to the thirteenth century, and in its current form it has been a staple of Lebanese, Syrian, Palestinian, and Israeli home cooking for as long as living memory extends. The version sold in British supermarkets is a pale approximation made with cheap tahini and too many chickpeas. The version made at home with dried chickpeas and good tahini is a different food.
